participants.reasoning: The extracted participants describe eligible acute stroke patients and participating stroke units in NSW acute stroke units, which aligns with the official study population and cluster settings.
participants.extracted: Patients admitted to any of the consenting 20 category A and B acute stroke units (ASUs) in New South Wales, Australia, were eligible to participate. At the patient level, the study recruited a consecutive sample of English-speaking adults aged over 18 years who presented within 48 hours of symptom onset and had a clinical diagnosis of ischaemic stroke or intracerebral haemorrhage confirmed by CT imaging. Patients were excluded if they presented 48 hours or more after symptom onset, had non-cerebrovascular causes of acute focal neurological deficits, sub-arachnoid haemorrhage, acute or chronic subdural haemorrhage, or required palliative care. At the cluster level, the participating units were the medical directors and nurse unit managers of eligible ASUs, who provided consent on behalf of their units and served as cluster guardians for the trial.

Intervention scope:stroke units at multiple (19) hospitals.
Health professionals:stroke team members ‐ including nurses, stroke unit coordinators, speech pathologists, physicians.
Patients:1696 (687 pre‐intervention, 1009 post‐intervention); acute stroke patients; 60% male, mean age not presented but between 65‐74, 95% ischaemic stroke, National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale not measured.
Type of acute stroke unit:18 acute intensive stroke units, 1 comprehensive stroke unit.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention components and control condition, including multidisciplinary workshops, protocols, education, reminders/support, and the abridged guidelines for controls.
interventions.extracted: The intervention was a cluster-level, multidisciplinary team-building program called the FeSS intervention, implemented in acute stroke units to improve evidence-based management of fever, hyperglycaemia, and swallowing dysfunction after stroke. It included development and dissemination of evidence-based clinical treatment protocols by expert panels, two onsite multidisciplinary workshops to identify local barriers and support adaptation, unit-based education sessions for nursing and speech pathology staff, ongoing proactive support and feedback from the project officer, and optional local audit tools to promote implementation. Control units received only an abridged version of the latest national stroke guidelines without additional implementation support.
interventions.official:
Intervention characteristics
Managing fever, hyperglycaemia and dysphage in acute stroke: The Quality in Acute Stroke Care (QASC) Trial
Barrier identification: Two multidisciplinary team‐building workshops to identify local barriers and enablers to implement the fever, sugar and swallowing dysfunction (FeSS) nurse‐initiated treatment protocols

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include death, disability, SF-36, Barthel Index, glucose, temperature, and swallowing screening, but the official outcomes also include aspiration pneumonia and length of hospital stay, while the extracted list adds stroke-related descriptions and clinician compliance not listed officially.
outcomes.extracted: Primary patient outcomes planned for measurement were: (1) death or disability at 90 days after hospital admission, with disability defined as Modified Rankin Scale (mRS) score ≥2; (2) level of disability at 90 days post-admission using the mRS (0–6 scale); (3) level of dependency at 90 days post-hospital admission using the Barthel Index (0–100 scale); and (4) health status at 90 days post-hospital admission using the SF-36, summarized as Physical Component Score (PCS) and Mental Component Score (MCS). Secondary behavior-change outcomes planned for measurement were: (1) glycaemic control measured by mean finger-prick blood glucose levels during the first 72 hours following admission to the acute stroke unit (ASU); (2) temperature control measured by mean temperature readings during the first 72 hours following admission to the ASU; and (3) management of swallowing dysfunction measured by whether swallowing screening was undertaken within the first 24 hours of admission to the ASU. In addition, the study planned to assess an overall clinician compliance measure based on the proportion of patients meeting applicable clinical care elements. Other clinical variables collected for description and potential confounding included stroke subtype using the Oxfordshire Community Stroke Project (OCSP) classification, stroke severity using the Scandinavian Stroke Scale (SSS) and/or Los Angeles Motor Scale (LAMS), premorbid disability using the mRS, and demographic variables (age, sex, date of admission, and length of stay), with patient clinical data supplemented from the TASC database or medical records as needed.
outcomes.official:
Data collected through chart audit by blinded data collectors:
Primary outcomes
Death or dependency (dependency: modified Rankin Scale ≥2) at 90 days
Functional dependency (Barthel index) at 90 days
Mean SF‐36 mental component summary score at 90 days
Mean physical component summary score at 90 days
Secondary outcomes
Mean temperature for the first 72 hours after acute stroke unit (ASU) admission
Mean finger‐prick blood glucose for the first 72 hours after ASU admission
Proportion with swallowin
